vectors.description: Sensitive data stored without encryption is exposed when storage media is stolen, improperly disposed, or accessed by unauthorized parties.
Data transmitted over unencrypted channels (HTTP, FTP, Telnet) can be intercepted via man-in-the-middle attacks or network sniffing.
Attackers exploit SQL injection vulnerabilities to extract database contents, bypassing application-level access controls.
Authorized users intentionally or accidentally transfer sensitive data outside the organization via email, USB, cloud storage, or printing.
Exposed cloud storage (S3 buckets, Azure blobs) or misconfigured API endpoints allowing unauthorized data access.
Stolen credentials used to access databases and data stores directly, bypassing application controls.
Data exposed through compromised vendors, partners, or service providers with access to organizational data.
Modern ransomware exfiltrates data before encrypting it (double extortion), selling or publishing stolen data.
Sensitive data on decommissioned hardware, returned leased equipment, or discarded documents is recovered by adversaries.
APIs returning excessive data (over-fetching), missing authentication, or lacking rate limiting expose sensitive data.
Unencrypted or poorly protected backup copies stored in accessible locations.
Production data used in development or testing environments with weaker security controls.
